name: Databases
description: Relational and NoSQL: modelling, SQL, transactions, indexing and distributed data. 30-question seed set, growing to 300.

questions.text: A primary key in a relational table…
options: Links to an external file
Encrypts the row's contents
Uniquely identifies each row
Sorts the table alphabetically
questions.explanation: One value (or set) that is unique and not null — the row's identity.

questions.text: SQL stands for…
options: System Query Layer
Sequential Query Logic
Structured Query Language
Simple Quick Lookup in practice
questions.explanation: The standard language for querying and manipulating relational databases.

questions.text: A foreign key is used to…
options: Encrypt data across tables
Speed up every query automatically
Link a row to a row in another table
Store the table's backup location
questions.explanation: It references another table's primary key, enforcing relationships between tables.

questions.text: The SQL command to retrieve rows is…
options: SELECT
READ
GET
FETCH in practice
questions.explanation: SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE is the workhorse of reading data.

questions.text: An index on a column mainly…
options: Speeds up lookups on that column
Prevents duplicate rows in practice
Compresses the whole table
Encrypts the column values
questions.explanation: Like a book index: faster reads, at the cost of extra storage and slower writes.

questions.text: The difference between SQL and NoSQL databases is that NoSQL…
options: Cannot store any structured data
Is always faster for every workload
Does not persist data to disk
Uses flexible, non-tabular data models
questions.explanation: Documents, key-value, graph, wide-column — models that trade rigid schemas for flexibility and scale.

questions.text: A NULL value in SQL represents…
options: The number zero
The absence of a value
A boolean false
An empty text string
questions.explanation: Unknown or missing — and it behaves specially: NULL = NULL is not true.

questions.text: A JOIN in SQL is used to…
options: Merge two databases into one
Add a new column to a table
Delete rows matching a condition in practice
Combine rows from two or more tables
questions.explanation: Relate tables on a matching column — inner, left, right and full variants differ in what they keep.

questions.text: A database transaction is…
options: A single row in a payments table
A backup taken every night
A connection from an application
A unit of work that succeeds or fails as a whole
questions.explanation: All-or-nothing: commit applies every change, rollback undoes them together.

questions.text: A schema in a relational database defines…
options: The physical disk layout
The structure: tables, columns and types
The query cache size
The user passwords in practice as a rule in practice
questions.explanation: The blueprint of how data is organised and constrained.

questions.text: The ACID property 'atomicity' guarantees that a transaction…
options: Runs faster than other transactions
Fully completes or has no effect at all
Survives a server crash once committed
Is isolated from all other transactions
questions.explanation: All steps or none — no half-applied transfers. (Durability is the crash-survival one.)

questions.text: A LEFT JOIN returns…
options: All rows from the left table, matched or not
Only rows matching in both tables
Rows from the right table only in practice as a rule
Only rows unique to the left table
questions.explanation: Every left row; right columns are NULL where there's no match.

questions.text: Normalisation aims to…
options: Increase the number of tables for fun in practice
Encrypt sensitive columns
Reduce redundancy and update anomalies
Speed up every read query
questions.explanation: Organise data so each fact lives once — fewer anomalies, at the cost of more joins.

questions.text: Denormalisation is sometimes used to…
options: Enforce stricter referential integrity
Guarantee ACID compliance
Reduce total storage used
Improve read performance by duplicating data
questions.explanation: Trade some redundancy for fewer joins when reads dominate and speed matters.

questions.text: A deadlock between two transactions happens when…
options: Each waits for a lock the other holds
The database runs out of connections
One transaction runs far too long in practice
Both finish at exactly the same time
questions.explanation: A cycle of waits; the database picks a victim to abort so the other can proceed.

questions.text: An B-tree index is well suited to…
options: Counting distinct values only
Range and equality lookups on ordered data
Full-text search across documents in practice
Storing binary large objects
questions.explanation: Balanced and ordered, it handles = and BETWEEN efficiently — the default index type.

questions.text: The N+1 query problem is…
options: Querying a table with N+1 columns in practice
Running N queries in parallel for speed
One query per row instead of a single join
A query that returns one extra row
questions.explanation: Fetch a list, then a query per item — death by round trips. Fix with a join or eager loading.

questions.text: A database view is…
options: An index on multiple columns
A physical copy of a table
A backup of the schema in practice as a rule
A saved query presented as a virtual table
questions.explanation: A named query you can select from like a table; it reflects live data unless materialised.

questions.text: Referential integrity ensures that…
options: Indexes stay perfectly balanced
A foreign key points to an existing row
All queries run inside a transaction
Every column has a default value
questions.explanation: You can't reference a customer that doesn't exist; the constraint blocks orphaned rows.

questions.text: A prepared statement helps performance and security by…
options: Encrypting the connection
Separating SQL structure from parameter values
Compressing the query text in practice as a rule
Caching the entire result set
questions.explanation: The plan is reused and, crucially, parameters can't become SQL — killing injection.

questions.text: In the CAP theorem, a distributed store under a network partition must trade off…
options: Storage against compute in practice
Security against speed
Consistency against availability
Cost against performance
questions.explanation: When partitioned, you either refuse some reads/writes (C) or serve possibly-stale ones (A) — you can't have both.

questions.text: A database isolation level of READ COMMITTED still permits…
options: Non-repeatable reads within a transaction
Lost updates only on primary keys in practice
Nothing; it is fully serialisable
Dirty reads of uncommitted data
questions.explanation: It blocks dirty reads but a row can change between two reads in the same transaction.

questions.text: MVCC (multi-version concurrency control) lets readers…
options: Always read the very latest write
Block all writers until they finish
Skip the transaction log entirely
See a consistent snapshot without blocking writers
questions.explanation: Readers see a snapshot, writers create new versions — high concurrency without read locks.

questions.text: Sharding a database means…
options: Partitioning data across multiple servers by key
Keeping full copies on every node in practice as a rule
Encrypting each table separately
Compressing old rows automatically
questions.explanation: Horizontal scale: each shard holds a slice keyed by, say, customer ID — but cross-shard joins get hard.

questions.text: A write-ahead log (WAL) provides durability by…
options: Writing changes to the log before applying them
Compressing data before storage
Replicating writes to every replica first in practice
Caching writes in memory indefinitely
questions.explanation: Record intent first; after a crash the log replays committed changes. The D in ACID, mechanically.

questions.text: Eventual consistency means that…
options: Consistency is checked once per day
Replicas converge given enough time without new writes
Data is never actually consistent
Every read always sees the latest write in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Accept temporary divergence for availability; absent new writes, replicas agree in the end.

questions.text: A covering index is one that…
options: Contains all columns a query needs
Is automatically created on primary keys
Spans multiple tables at once
Covers every column in the table
questions.explanation: The query is answered from the index alone — no trip to the table (an 'index-only scan').

questions.text: Optimistic locking detects conflicts by…
options: Locking rows before anyone reads them
Serialising every write globally
Blocking all concurrent transactions
Checking a version/timestamp at write time
questions.explanation: Assume no clash; on commit, if the version changed underneath you, reject and retry.

questions.text: A materialised view differs from a normal view because it…
options: Exists only in memory
Stores the query result and must be refreshed
Always reflects live data instantly in practice
Cannot be queried like a table
questions.explanation: Precomputed and persisted for speed — at the cost of staleness until you refresh it.

questions.text: Connection pooling improves throughput by…
options: Encrypting every database connection
Sharding data across pools in practice as a rule in practice
Reusing established connections instead of reopening
Caching query results in the client
questions.explanation: Opening a connection is expensive; a pool hands out and recycles a fixed set, smoothing load spikes.
